items.title: USDA finalizes stricter SNAP retailer stocking standards; enforcement begins this fall
items.summary: USDA’s final rule raises the minimum staple-food varieties a SNAP-authorized retailer must stock from 3 to 7 in each of the four staple-food categories, and increases the perishable requirement from 2 to 3 categories. The rule takes effect July 7, 2026 and retailers must comply no later than Nov. 4, 2026, which could affect which small stores can stay SNAP-authorized in some areas.

items.title: USDA allows whole and 2% milk again for kids age 2+ in school meals and CACFP
items.summary: A USDA final rule allows whole and reduced-fat (2%) milk to be offered to participants age 2 and up in the National School Lunch Program, School Breakfast Program, CACFP, and the Special Milk Program. The rule is effective June 8, 2026, and also clarifies that children ages 2–5 may be served unflavored whole, 2%, 1%, or fat-free milk.

items.title: Summer EBT (SUN Bucks) delivers $120 per eligible child for groceries during summer break
items.summary: USDA notes that in many states and some Tribal Nations, eligible children can receive Summer EBT grocery benefits of $120 per child for the summer. Families can also use USDA’s site finder and National Hunger Hotline (1-866-348-6479) to locate Summer meal sites and other food resources.

items.title: SSA posts June and July 2026 payment dates: SSI on the 1st; Social Security on Wednesdays
items.summary: SSA’s 2026–2027 calendar shows SSI payments scheduled for June 1 and July 1, 2026. For Social Security beneficiaries who started benefits before May 1997 (or who receive both Social Security and SSI), Social Security is paid June 3 and July 3; other beneficiaries are paid June 10/17/24 and July 8/15/22 depending on birthdate.

items.title: Taxpayer Advocate warns some taxpayers may need to file protective refund claims by July 10, 2026
items.summary: The Taxpayer Advocate Service says some taxpayers who believe they missed refund opportunities for tax years 2019–2022 may need to file a refund claim or a protective claim on or before July 10, 2026, due to ongoing litigation around disaster-related filing postponements. TAS emphasizes the law is unsettled and taxpayers should not assume the IRS will automatically allow Kwong-based claims.

items.title: IRS: new child savings Trump Accounts can’t be funded until July 4, 2026; government adds $1,000 per eligible child
items.summary: IRS guidance says Trump Accounts for eligible children cannot be funded before July 4, 2026, and the federal government will make a one-time $1,000 contribution per eligible child. Individuals and employers can contribute up to $5,000 per year, and employers may contribute up to $2,500 per year without it counting as taxable income for the employee.

items.title: 2026–27 Pell Grant maximum award is $7,395 as new aid year starts July 1
items.summary: The 2026–2027 federal student aid award year starts July 1, 2026, and the maximum Federal Pell Grant for that year is $7,395. Students who need aid for summer 2026 (July 1, 2025–June 30, 2026) can still submit the 2025–26 FAFSA before the federal deadline.
